attributes.stem:
An 82-year-old man is admitted to the palliative care ward for end-of-life care. He has metastatic bladder cancer with bone and liver metastases. His medical history includes ischaemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation, and type 2 diabetes. His regular medications have been ceased except for a subcutaneous syringe driver containing morphine and midazolam.

On examination, he appears comfortable but drowsy. His vital signs show a pulse rate of 88/min and respiratory rate of 16/min. He is minimally responsive to voice, with occasional periods of restlessness that settle with breakthrough midazolam. He has dry oral mucosa and reduced skin turgor. His family is concerned about his reduced oral intake and potential discomfort from dehydration.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following statements regarding hydration in this dying patient is most appropriate?
options: Artificial hydration can reduce terminal delirium and restlessness
Intravenous fluids would cause discomfort in end of life
Reduced oral intake is a natural part of the dying process
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a common situation in end-of-life care where families express concern about reduced oral intake and hydration. The physiological changes that occur during the dying process naturally lead to decreased fluid requirements, and there is no evidence that artificial hydration prolongs survival or improve dehydration symptoms. Communication with families regarding the natural and expected progression of their loved one through the process of dying may improve their understanding and reduce distress during this sensitive time.

Reduced oral intake is a natural part of the dying process. As the body systems begin to shut down, there is decreased need for fluids and nutrition. This process is protective - it can reduce pulmonary secretions, decrease gastrointestinal fluid, reduce urine output, and decrease peripheral oedema. The resulting mild ketosis may also have an analgesic effect.

Artificial hydration does not reduce terminal delirium or restlessness. In fact, terminal agitation is often multifactorial and may be exacerbated by fluid administration through increased secretions and peripheral oedema. Delirium in the terminal phase is better managed with appropriate medications such as antipsychotics or benzodiazepines.

Intravenous fluids in end-of-life care don't directly cause discomfort, but could contribute to fluid overload, leading to increased respiratory secretions, peripheral oedema, and potentially worsening any pleural effusions or ascites. The discomfort of maintaining intravenous access must also be considered.

Subcutaneous fluids do not prolong survival in the terminal phase. While hypodermoclysis may occasionally be considered for specific reversible causes of confusion, there is no evidence that it extends life or improves quality of death.

Fluid replacement does not reduce overall symptom burden in the terminal phase. Studies have shown that terminal dehydration does not cause suffering and may actually be more comfortable for the patient.

The key to management is providing appropriate education and support to the family about the natural dying process. This includes explaining that reduced intake is expected and not harmful, and focusing on comfort measures such as good mouth care and lip moistening to address the visible signs of dry mouth that often concern families.
learningPoints: Terminal dehydration is a natural part of the dying process and does not cause distress to the patient.
Artificial hydration in end-of-life care may increase secretions and worsen respiratory symptoms.
Family education about the natural dying process is crucial for acceptance and understanding.
references: Lokker ME, Van der Heide A, Oldenmenger WH, Van der Rijt CC, Van Zuylen L. Hydration and symptoms in the last days of life. BMJ supportive & palliative care. 2021 Sep 1;11(3):335-43. Available online at ; https://spcare.bmj.com/content/bmjspcare/11/3/335.full.pdf

Wu CY, Chen PJ, Ho TL, Lin WY, Cheng SY. To hydrate or not to hydrate? The effect of hydration on survival, symptoms and quality of dying among terminally ill cancer patients. BMC palliative care. 2021 Dec;20:1-0. Available online at ; https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1186/s12904-021-00710-9.pdf
